body_txt: The Generic Doomsday Villain is an overpowering antagonist who is not given any defined personality, characterisation, or motive behind their destructive actions. They're one of the most basic story villains possible. They are a Flat Character who exists solely to be an obstacle to the heroes, with the reasoning behind their actions being unknown or non-existent. Their Establishing Character Moment is generally the most development that they get, designed to show the audience what sort of threat that they're facing. The character may or may not provide an explanation of their Evil Plan , but their overall goal is some sort of Apocalypse How , providing the "doomsday". At the very least, they plan on destroying the people within the town/country that the heroes are from. When part of a recurring work, this character is forced into one of two outcomes: they either remain a Static Character who has almost nothing aside from " I want to destroy everything ", or they become a Dynamic Character and lose their "generic" quality. Of course, Tropes Are Tools , and some writers intentionally make these characters flat to highlight their unhealthy psyche. Alternately, in some situations, they may be a Silent Antagonist and barely tell the audience anything at all. Often the other characters won't be able to bother with the why , and know they just have to stop the villain at all costs. In other stories, they are flat because they are more of a super-powered Right-Hand Attack Dog and/or a Greater-Scope Villain being used by a villain with proper characterization and motives. An Eldritch Abomination is likely to be this; a godlike entity whose motives are a complete mystery, and who never speaks in an identifiable human language, with this being done to make them inhuman and scary. This type of villain is especially common for video games, as they exist more as obstacles for the players to defeat than characters to interact with; often this overlaps with a Giant Space Flea from Nowhere . Most recurring Slasher Movie villains or giant monster antagonists start off this way, as the point of the work is to be scary. Tropes Are Tools and nothing scares people more than the unknown. Instead, the emphasis is placed on the victims or other characters. Contrast with Complete Monster , who may not have a plan at all, but does have plenty of characterization and an established motivation, and Tragic Villain , whose sad backstory is arguably the most important part of their characterization. Compare Feral Villain , where the character has no sapience with which to have characterization, Hidden Agenda Villain who has non-generic motives but they're hidden from audiences, and "Clichéd Villain" Accusation and Disappointed by the Motive in-universe accusations of such. noreallife
